message.content: What about registry and policy, registry could fit into pool

content.text:
Both were consciously left out of this cycle — here's the reasoning, and my take on the pool suggestion:

**Policy** is shared-core, not a primitive. `policy.rs` holds wait-policy helpers (`policy::spin`) consumed by both ring flavors' endpoints — the same category as `Error`, `Full`/`Empty`, `slot_ptr` in `lib.rs`. There's no protocol in it to fork into a `v1`, so it has no version axis; versioning it would just add a directory level with nothing to select between.

**Registry** — the In Progress decisions block explicitly deferred it: "registry stays top-level — its placement waits for the descriptor-queue endpoints work." On "registry could fit into pool": it's genuinely pool-adjacent (it maps `PoolId` → pool regions, and its one dependency is `pool::PoolResolver`), so nesting it under `pool/` isn't wrong today. But I'd stick with the deferral, for two reasons:

- The consumer that will actually shape it is Todo #1, the descriptor-queue endpoints (`DescSender`/`DescReceiver`), which own "ring endpoint + registry access" and are designed against both ring flavors. That work may want registry as a peer of the messaging layer (rings + pools + descriptors) rather than inside one primitive — deciding now pre-empts a design that's one cycle away.
- If registry did move under `pool/`, the follow-on question is whether it goes in `pool/v0/` (versioned with the pool's layout) or `pool/` (unversioned) — and that's exactly the coupling question the descriptor work will answer, since `Desc` resolution spans pool layout and queue protocol.

So my recommendation: land `-3` as-is, keep both top-level, and let the descriptor-queue cycle place registry with full information. If you'd rather fold registry under `pool/` now, it's a small mechanical addition to this commit — your call.

# Chores-02

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

Continues [chores-01.md](chores-01.md), opened as that file
approached ~1000 lines. Reference numbering restarts at `[1]`
(file-local — see
[Reference numbering](../../AGENTS.md#reference-numbering)).

## docs: execution contexts + ISR blue-sky goal

Commits: [[1]]

Design-doc pass out of conversation (no ladder): the
arbitrary-graph topology bullet, the Execution contexts
subsection (thread/ISR sole-owner and sharing cases), the
blue-sky ISR-to-ISR goal with its gotchas, and the
2t-surprise cache-line-bounce mechanism sharpened in
[chores-01](chores-01.md#outcome-the-2t-surprise).

## refactor: versioned primitive module dirs

Commits:

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/2ea448654c9a "2ea448654c9a4b7f758e017d56161d9d731ab425"

1	# Ring buffer design
2	
3	Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
4	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers terminology,
5	requirements, constraints, the memory layout, the API, and
6	the validation ladder, and is kept in sync with the
7	implementation in `src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the
8	region-level machinery (header, init/attach/split, geometry
9	helpers) and re-exports the endpoint modules `producer.rs`
10	(Producer, WriteSlot, Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer,
11	ReadSlot, Empty).
12	
13	## Goal
14	
15	A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
16	a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
17	its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
18	in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
19	inter-process (shared memory).
20

file.content:
# Ring buffer design

Design notes for the zero-copy ring buffer. This file uses
[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It covers terminology,
requirements, constraints, the memory layout, the API, and
the validation ladder, and is kept in sync with the
implementation in `src/` (as-built): `lib.rs` holds the
region-level machinery (header, init/attach/split, geometry
helpers) and re-exports the endpoint modules `producer.rs`
(Producer, WriteSlot, Full) and `consumer.rs` (Consumer,
ReadSlot, Empty).

## Goal

A ring buffer for passing typed messages between a producer and
a consumer with no copying at the boundary: the producer writes
its message directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads it
in place. The same layout must work intra-process (threads) and
inter-process (shared memory).

content.content:
77	1. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
78	   send) / DescReceiver (recv) [[11]]:
79	   - own ring endpoint + registry access;
80	   - the demo's ~20-line send path becomes ~3 lines;
81	   - `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once inside the crate
82	     (recv safe by construction);
83	   - guard handed back on Full;
84	   - design against both ring flavors (SPSC + MPSC);
85	   - the sender is also where each sender's private
86	     overflow pending list will live.
87	2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
88	   sender-private pending list instead of failing
89	   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
90	   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
91	     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
92	   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
93	   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
94	     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
95	3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
96	   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
97	   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
98	   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
99	     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
100	     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
101	   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
102	     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
103	   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
104	     into the SPSC protocol.
105	4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
106	   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
107	   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
108	   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
109	   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
110	   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
111	   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
112	   demo should show it.
113	5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
114	   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
115	   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
116	   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
117	   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
118	6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
119	   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
120	   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).
121	
122	## Ideas
123	
124	- Perf benches live in
125	  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
126	  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
127	  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
128	  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
129	  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
130	  proves necessary.
131	
132	- Fan-in helper: consumer-side composition polling N SPSC
133	  rings under a pluggable service policy (priority,
134	  round-robin, weighted)
135	  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode):
136	  - buildable today from shipped parts;
137	  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no
138	    commitment yet.
139	- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
140	  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
141	  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
142	  this project in
143	  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
144	- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
145	  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
146	  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
147	  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
148	  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
149	  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
150	  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
151	  iiac-perf first.
152	- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
153	  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
154	  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
155	  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
156	  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
157	  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
158	  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
159	  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
160	  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
161	  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
162	- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
163	  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
164	  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
165	  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
166	  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
167	  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
168	- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
169	  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
170	  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
171	  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
172	  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
173	  upgrade than to walk back.
174	- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
175	  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
176	  contracts in
177	  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
178	  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
179	  share one protocol.
180	- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
181	  protocol.
182	- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
183	  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
184	- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
185	  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
186	- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
187	  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
188	  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
189	  values from iiac-perf measurements.
190	- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
191	  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
192	  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
193	  targets would need index-width genericization.
194	- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
195	  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
196	  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
197	- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
198	  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
199	  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
200	  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
201	  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
202	  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).
203	
204	## Done
205	
206	Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed

   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Fan-in helper: consumer-side composition polling N SPSC
  rings under a pluggable service policy (priority,
  round-robin, weighted)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode):
  - buildable today from shipped parts;
  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no
    commitment yet.
- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed

input.old_string:
## In Progress

**refactor: versioned primitive module dirs**

The three primitives sit at uneven depths — `mpsc/` is a
directory, SPSC is spread across `lib.rs` plus top-level
`producer.rs` / `consumer.rs`, and the pool is one
`pool.rs` — and there is nowhere for a second implementation
of a primitive to live for A/B comparison in iiac-perf. Give
each primitive a `{primitive}/v0/` module dir so all three
are siblings and a future `v1` slots in beside `v0` as a
peer.

Decisions (the versioning discussion, folded in):

- **Structure** — `src/spsc/v0/`, `src/mpsc/v0/`,
  `src/pool/v0/`; version is the inner axis, so each
  primitive versions independently.
- **Names unchanged** — keep the current type names inside
  `v0` (`Ring`, `MpscRing`, `Pool`, …); a default-version
  re-export per module (`spsc::Ring = spsc::v0::Ring`) plus
  the existing crate-root re-exports keep the public API
  identical, so the cycle is a pure move. `spsc::v0::Ring` is
  newly reachable for explicit version pinning.
- **Shared core stays in `lib.rs`** — `Error`,
  `CACHE_LINE_SIZE`, `USER_WORDS`, `slot_ptr`, `check_type`,
  `CacheAligned` are crate-wide; versions reach them via
  `crate::`.
- **`Full` / `Empty` promote to the shared core** — defined
  in the SPSC endpoint files today but imported by mpsc
  (`crate::{Empty, Full}`); leaving them inside `spsc/v0/`
  would couple sibling primitives (and any future
  `spsc::v1`) to one version's module. Crate-root re-exports
  keep the public API unchanged.
- **`-1` also moves the SPSC core out of `lib.rs`** —
  `Header`, `Ring`, `MAGIC`, `LAYOUT_VERSION`, `header_ptr`,
  `region_size`, `validate_geometry` go into `spsc/v0/`
  alongside `producer.rs` / `consumer.rs`.
- **registry stays top-level** — its placement waits for the
  descriptor-queue endpoints work.
- **Cargo feature-gating deferred** — coexistence needs no
  features; per-version build-size exclusion only pays off
  once a `v1` exists, so features land with the first fork.
- **Close-out doc sync** — `ring-buffer-design.md`'s
  as-built intro names top-level `producer.rs` /
  `consumer.rs`; sync it to the new layout at close-out.

Plan ladder:

- `0.12.0-0` chore: open versioned module dir refactor
  (done)
- `0.12.0-1` refactor: spsc into a v0 module dir (done)
- `0.12.0-2` refactor: mpsc into a v0 module dir (done)
- `0.12.0-3` refactor: pool into a v0 module dir (done)
- `0.12.0` refactor: versioned primitive module dirs

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) [[11]]:
   - own ring endpoint + registry access;
   - the demo's ~20-line send path becomes ~3 lines;
   - `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once inside the crate
     (recv safe by construction);
   - guard handed back on Full;
   - design against both ring flavors (SPSC + MPSC);
   - the sender is also where each sender's private
     overflow pending list will live.
2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
   sender-private pending list instead of failing
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking

  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)

  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from

- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:

- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack

- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the

  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) [[11]]:
   - own ring endpoint + registry access;
   - the demo's ~20-line send path becomes ~3 lines;
   - `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once inside the crate
     (recv safe by construction);
   - guard handed back on Full;
   - design against both ring flavors (SPSC + MPSC);
   - the sender is also where each sender's private
     overflow pending list will live.
2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
   sender-private pending list instead of failing
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Fan-in helper: consumer-side composition polling N SPSC
  rings under a pluggable service policy (priority,
  round-robin, weighted)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode):
  - buildable today from shipped parts;
  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no
    commitment yet.
- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

## refactor: versioned primitive module dirs

Commits:

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/2ea448654c9a "2ea448654c9a4b7f758e017d56161d9d731ab425"
input.new_string:
## refactor: versioned primitive module dirs

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]]

The three primitives sat at uneven depths — `mpsc/` was a
directory, SPSC was spread across `lib.rs` plus top-level
`producer.rs` / `consumer.rs`, and the pool was one
`pool.rs` — and there was nowhere for a second implementation
of a primitive to live for A/B comparison in iiac-perf. Each
primitive now has a `{primitive}/v0/` module dir so all three
are siblings and a future `v1` slots in beside `v0` as a
peer.

Decisions (the versioning discussion, folded in):

- **Structure** — `src/spsc/v0/`, `src/mpsc/v0/`,
  `src/pool/v0/`; version is the inner axis, so each
  primitive versions independently.
- **Names unchanged** — keep the current type names inside
  `v0` (`Ring`, `MpscRing`, `Pool`, …); a default-version
  re-export per module (`spsc::Ring = spsc::v0::Ring`) plus
  the existing crate-root re-exports keep the public API
  identical, so the cycle is a pure move. `spsc::v0::Ring` is
  newly reachable for explicit version pinning.
- **Shared core stays in `lib.rs`** — `Error`,
  `CACHE_LINE_SIZE`, `USER_WORDS`, `slot_ptr`, `check_type`,
  `CacheAligned` are crate-wide; versions reach them via
  `crate::`.
- **`Full` / `Empty` promote to the shared core** — they were
  defined in the SPSC endpoint files but imported by mpsc
  (`crate::{Empty, Full}`); leaving them inside `spsc/v0/`
  would couple sibling primitives (and any future
  `spsc::v1`) to one version's module. Crate-root re-exports
  keep the public API unchanged.
- **`-1` also moves the SPSC core out of `lib.rs`** —
  `Header`, `Ring`, `MAGIC`, `LAYOUT_VERSION`, `header_ptr`,
  `region_size`, `validate_geometry` into `spsc/v0/`
  alongside `producer.rs` / `consumer.rs`.
- **registry and policy stay top-level** — policy is shared
  core (no version axis); registry's placement waits for the
  descriptor-queue endpoints work.
- **Cargo feature-gating deferred** — coexistence needs no
  features; per-version build-size exclusion only pays off
  once a `v1` exists, so features land with the first fork.
- **Wording: "default-version re-export", not "champion
  alias"** — the `-1` docstrings coined "champion alias" (a
  blend of champion–challenger testing and a Rust
  re-export); `-2` swept it for plain searchable terms.

### As-built ladder

- `0.12.0-0` chore: open versioned module dir refactor
- `0.12.0-1` refactor: spsc into a v0 module dir
- `0.12.0-2` refactor: mpsc into a v0 module dir
  (also the champion-alias wording sweep)
- `0.12.0-3` refactor: pool into a v0 module dir
- `0.12.0` refactor: versioned primitive module dirs
  (close-out: bookkeeping + design-doc as-built sync; its
  SHA backfills on the next push)

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/2ea448654c9a "2ea448654c9a4b7f758e017d56161d9d731ab425"
[2]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/f2b6384a42b6 "f2b6384a42b6a348c28cfb10a8b8900c4f43fbb5"
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/6daa99e3a0f8 "6daa99e3a0f81df6fca2c4b2e178e9d4ccaa8180"
[4]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/9e25f853111d "9e25f853111d5757dc16fae8401dbdc0fe10fff6"
[5]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/423ac89b4abb "423ac89b4abb1c9330c30724cb9d1256cf95b510"

# Chores-02

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks

content.text:
Validated against GitHub, and the current reference is actually correct there — the proposed change would break it:

- The heading `### Follow-on: endpoints and wait policies` renders on github.com with anchor id `user-content-follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies` (fetched just now from the live page). That matches the existing `[11]: chores/chores-01.md#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies`, and also the in-file link at chores-01.md:458 which uses the same slug.
- This agrees with the algorithm recorded in AGENTS.md: the colon is *stripped*, not converted — only its trailing space becomes a hyphen, so no doubling.
- A detail worth noting: colon-to-hyphen conversion would produce `follow-on--endpoints-...` (double hyphen *after* "on", where the colon sits), but your working link had `follow--on-...` (double hyphen between "follow" and "on") — so whatever renderer accepted that is doing something else again, possibly treating the existing hyphen in "Follow-on" specially.

So we have a renderer discrepancy, not a broken ref: GitHub wants the current single-hyphen form. Where did the single-hyphen link fail for you — Zed's markdown preview, VS Code, or somewhere else? If it's the editor preview, the refs should stay GitHub-canonical (that's what the notes conventions target) and we'd just note the editor quirk; changing the ref would fix the preview but break the published page.

message.content: I tested it on github just now and you're correct [11] does work. It's failing on zed preview but does work on vscode, so bug in zed!

content.text:
Good — mystery resolved: the refs are GitHub-canonical and correct, and Zed's markdown preview is generating non-standard anchors (its slugger apparently treats the existing hyphen in "Follow-on" differently, producing that `follow--on` form).

Two optional follow-ups, your call:

- Add a one-line caveat to AGENTS.md's "Markdown anchor links" section noting Zed's preview uses a different slugger, so a link that 404s in Zed isn't necessarily broken — GitHub is the reference. Cheap insurance against re-chasing this later (by either of us).
- Report it upstream to Zed (zed-industries/zed) — a minimal repro is easy: a heading with a hyphenated word before a colon.

message.content:
You say "Zed's markdown preview generates different anchors for headings"
that isn't correct we generate the anchors.

Actually, there is a `[n]` in the body and then of a corresponding left
justified "thing" of the form `[n]: urlish`, please help with the
terminology.

content.text:
Right — good catch on who does what. Two things happen: **we** write the fragment (`#follow-on-...`) into the notes, and the **renderer** auto-generates an id for each heading and then matches our fragment against it. GitHub and VS Code generate github-slugger-style ids so our fragment resolves; Zed's preview evidently generates (or matches) heading ids differently, so the same fragment finds nothing there. The caveat should say that.

On terminology, CommonMark has exact names for both pieces:

- `[n]` in the body is a **reference link** — specifically a *shortcut reference link* (just the label; `[text][n]` with separate text is a *full reference link*). Our doubled `[[n]]` form is what AGENTS.md calls a *citation* — a literal `[` + the reference link + `]`.
- The left-justified `[n]: urlish "title"` line is a **link reference definition**. Its parts: `[n]` is the *link label*, the urlish part is the *link destination*, and the optional quoted string is the *link title* (where we stash the 40-hex SHA in commit refs).
- Within the destination, the `#...` part is the **fragment** (fragment identifier), and the auto-generated heading id it must match is the **heading anchor** (the slug).

Here's the corrected caveat text:

> Renderer caveat: our link reference definitions write GitHub-style fragments (`#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies`); each renderer auto-generates its own heading anchors and matches the fragment against them. As of July 2026 that resolves on GitHub and in VS Code (both verified) but 404s in Zed's markdown preview, whose generated anchors differ. A fragment that fails only in Zed's preview is not broken; GitHub is the reference.

1. Start Zed with a clean install.
2. Create a new file: test.md
3. Paste the following Markdown:

```

   https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/issues/1

   Markdown link:
   [Zed repo](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed)

   Internal project link:
   [README](./README.md)
```
4. Open Markdown Preview.
5. Hover over each link (cursor changes to pointer).
6. Click the links.


### Current vs. Expected behavior

Current behavior:
- All links are styled as clickable (blue text, underline on hover, pointer cursor).
- Clicking any link (external or internal) does nothing.
- No navigation occurs.
- No error message or feedback is shown.

Expected behavior:
- Clicking external links should open the system browser.
- Clicking internal project links (e.g., ./README.md) should open the file in Zed.
- If link navigation is intentionally blocked, the preview should not render links as interactive UI elements.

content.text:
No existing issue matches — here's what I found in the Zed repo:

**Issue search:** Nothing on anchor/slug mismatches. The closest are #18699 (the feature request for clickable heading anchors, closed by the implementation) and #58270 (preview links doing nothing at all on Windows — a different failure).

**But the source complicates the story.** The anchor feature landed 2026-04-09 via PR #53184, and the slugger currently on `main` (`generate_heading_slug` in `crates/util/src/markdown.rs`) keeps hyphens, *drops* colons, and maps spaces to hyphens — which produces exactly the GitHub slug `follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies` for our heading. Its unit tests even pin `"Hello-World"` → `"hello-world"`. So by the current code, the canonical fragment should resolve in Zed.

That leaves two candidate explanations for what you saw:

- **Your installed Zed predates or diverges from that code** — worth checking your version against April 2026.
- **The bug is in fragment *matching*, not slug generation** — our `[11]` is a cross-file reference (`chores/chores-01.md#...` — note: relative subdir path without a `./` prefix, where PR #53184's stated test case was `./other.md#heading`). The generation side being correct doesn't rule out the lookup side mangling the comparison.

One detail that doesn't fit any theory yet: a colon-to-hyphen conversion would yield `follow-on--endpoints-...` (double hyphen where the colon was), but the variant you reported working was `follow--on-...` (double hyphen inside "Follow-on"). If that wasn't a typo, Zed is doing something to the pre-existing hyphen itself, which no version of their slugger I can see explains.

To file a solid issue I'd want: your Zed version (`zed --version` or the About dialog), and whether the failure reproduces with a *same-file* link (`[x](#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies)` inside chores-01.md itself) or only cross-file. Want to grab those two data points, or shall I draft the issue with what we have and flag the unknowns?

content.text:
Here are the links, pinned to your v1.9.0:

- **Slug generation** — [`crates/util/src/markdown.rs#L3-L17`](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/util/src/markdown.rs#L3-L17), `generate_heading_slug`. This is the file I meant. **Most recent change: 2026-04-09**, commit [`b150663d45`](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/commit/b150663d45) from PR [#53184](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/pull/53184) — the commit that introduced the whole anchor feature. Nothing has touched the file since, so v1.9.0 ships exactly what's on `main`.
- **Heading→slug table construction** — [`crates/markdown/src/parser.rs#L100`](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown/src/parser.rs#L100), `build_heading_slugs` (accumulates heading text, calls the slugger, handles duplicate-heading `-1` suffixes).
- **Fragment lookup / scroll** — [`crates/markdown/src/markdown.rs#L686`](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown/src/markdown.rs#L686), `scroll_to_heading`, and the click path that splits URL from fragment at [`crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L862`](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L862).

The puzzle deepens, though: reading the v1.9.0 code, your heading is plain text with no inline formatting, and `generate_heading_slug("Follow-on: endpoints and wait policies")` provably returns `follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies` — the canonical slug. Generation-side, v1.9.0 looks correct. So if it 404s for you on 1.9, the defect is most likely in the lookup/navigation path (the `scroll_to_heading` / fragment-split links above), or specific to cross-file links — our ref's destination is `chores/chores-01.md#...`, a subdir-relative path without the `./` prefix that PR #53184's test plan used. A quick discriminating test when you're poking at it: put `[test](#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies)` *inside* chores-01.md itself — if that works in Zed's preview while the cross-file `[11]` ref fails, it's the cross-file path handling, not the slugger.

content.text: The working double-dash link is actually strong evidence about *generation*, not search: `scroll_to_heading` does a plain HashMap lookup, so if the double-dash fragment scrolls, the table genuinely contains the double-dash slug — meaning the slugger, as executed on whatever text it receives, produced it. Let me read the part of `build_heading_slugs` I skipped — how the heading text accumulates across parser events:

input.description: Show text accumulation arm of build_heading_slugs

content.text:
That code settles the search-side theory, actually — and points the finger back at generation. Here's the chain:

- The click handler (`markdown_preview_view.rs:867`) takes the fragment verbatim and `scroll_to_heading` looks it up in the `heading_slugs` HashMap — an exact string match, no colon handling anywhere. The search can't "mishandle" a colon; it either finds the key or doesn't.
- So if adding the extra dash makes it scroll, the HashMap key *is* the double-dash string — Zed's table was built with that slug. The bug is in what `build_heading_slugs` + `generate_heading_slug` produced, not in the matching.
- Which is genuinely weird, because the accumulation you see above concatenates raw source slices (`heading_text.push_str(&source[range])`), so `heading_text` should be exactly `Follow-on: endpoints and wait policies`, and the v1.9.0 slugger provably drops the colon and yields the canonical single-dash slug. Reading the code, this bug shouldn't exist — yet it does on your machine. One suspect visible here: the `SubstitutedText` arm (parser.rs:152) pushes *substituted* text instead of source text — if some substitution fires inside your heading, the slugger sees altered input.

There's one measurement that would pin it down, and only you can take it — the exact position of the working extra dash. Two candidates, and they implicate different code:

1. `#follow-on--endpoints-and-wait-policies` (double dash where the colon was) → the colon is being *converted* to a dash rather than dropped — slugger input or a stale/divergent slugger.
2. `#follow--on-endpoints-and-wait-policies` (double dash inside "Follow-on") → something is mangling the pre-existing hyphen — much stranger, likely that `SubstitutedText` path.

Earlier you typed variant 2, but a typo would be easy. Could you check which of the two actually scrolls in Zed 1.9? With that answer I can draft a precise upstream issue — minimal repro (`### Follow-on: endpoints and wait policies` + both fragment variants), your version, and the code-path analysis above.

content.text:
Empirical confirmation on 1.10 — even better for the report. Here's the draft issue:

---

**Title:** Markdown preview: cross-file anchor links open the file but discard the `#fragment`

**Body:**

### Summary

Clicking a cross-file anchor link (`[text](other.md#some-heading)`) in the Markdown preview opens the target file, but never scrolls to the heading — the fragment is silently dropped. Same-file anchor links (`[text](#some-heading)`) work correctly.

PR #53184 (which added anchor-link support, and closed #18699) states in its description that cross-file anchor links should "open the file, scroll the preview, and move the editor cursor to the heading" — that last part doesn't happen.

### Reproduction steps

1. Create `a.md`:
   ```markdown
   [go to section](b.md#section-two)
   ```
2. Create `b.md`:
   ```markdown
   # Section one

   (enough lines to require scrolling)

   # Section two
   ```
3. Open `a.md`'s Markdown preview and click the link.

### Current vs. expected behavior

- Current: `b.md` opens, positioned at the top; the `#section-two` fragment is ignored.
- Expected: `b.md` opens scrolled to "Section two" (per PR #53184's description).

### Cause

In `crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs`, `handle_url_click` routes any link with a non-empty path part to `open_preview_url`, whose first line splits the fragment off and discards it:

```rust
let (path_text, _) = split_preview_url(url.as_ref());
```

No `scroll_to_heading` is attempted after the file opens. The same-file branch (empty path part) does call `scroll_to_heading`, which is why same-file anchors work. Verified present in v1.9.0 ([L1008 equivalent](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L903)) and v1.10.0 (L1008); behavior confirmed on both.

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.0 (also reproduced on v1.9.0)
OS: Linux (Arch, kernel 7.1.3-arch1-1)

---

Want any edits, or should I file it under your account with `gh`? (You may want your exact GPU/memory specs filled in from `zed: copy system specs` — the template asks for them, though for this bug they're irrelevant.)

content.text:
Nice bit of archaeology — that likely explains why nobody *noticed* (they don't dogfood numbered link reference definitions in their own docs), but I don't think it changes the mechanism. Two reasons:

- Your reference links *do* work in Zed except for the fragment — the file opens. So Zed parses `[11]` → `[11]: chores/chores-01.md#...` fine (pulldown-cmark resolves reference definitions before Zed ever sees the link; by the time `handle_url_click` runs, a reference link and an inline link are the same resolved URL string).
- The discard in `open_preview_url` happens on that resolved URL, so it should hit **inline** cross-file anchors (`[text](b.md#section-two)`) just as hard. Link style shouldn't matter.

That said, my drafted repro uses an inline link and I've only actually *observed* the failure through your reference links — worth 30 seconds to close the gap. If you paste `[go](chores/chores-01.md#follow-on-endpoints-and-wait-policies)` inline into notes/todo.md and click it in the preview:

- **Opens without scrolling** → analysis holds, the repro is maximally general, and your grep finding becomes a nice "why this went unnoticed" footnote for the issue.
- **Scrolls correctly** → link style *does* matter somehow, my code reading missed something, and the issue should be reframed around reference-style links specifically.

Which is it?

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) [[11]]:
   - own ring endpoint + registry access;
   - the demo's ~20-line send path becomes ~3 lines;
   - `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once inside the crate
     (recv safe by construction);
   - guard handed back on Full;
   - design against both ring flavors (SPSC + MPSC);
   - the sender is also where each sender's private
     overflow pending list will live.
2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
   sender-private pending list instead of failing
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Fan-in helper: consumer-side composition polling N SPSC
  rings under a pluggable service policy (priority,
  round-robin, weighted)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode):
  - buildable today from shipped parts;
  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no
    commitment yet.
- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

   - the sender is also where each sender's private

2. Overflow FIFO: on ring Full, append the message to a
   sender-private pending list instead of failing
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future):
   - intrusive — the same embedded next-link the
     free-stack uses, so zero allocation;
   - naturally bounded by pool capacity;
   - composes per-sender with MPSC — see
     [Overflow readiness](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-readiness).
3. Explore why zcr-mpsc-2t measures faster than zcr-with-2t
   (73.9 vs 100.1 ns adjusted mean at 300s, 1p/1c) and
   whether the mechanism can improve SPSC [[20]]:
   - we think SPSC bounces two index lines per handoff
     (each side polls the line the other writes) while
     MPSC's only shared hot word is the slot seq;
   - verify with perf cache-transfer counters and/or the
     padded-seq variant (a design open question);
   - if confirmed, a seam-word variant might feed back
     into the SPSC protocol.
4. Batch alloc/free demo: alongside the one-message
   alloc_free_1t loops, a variant that allocs X messages
   (5, 10, …) then frees them all, pool vs global
   allocator. We think the pool's rate stays constant
   (pop/push is O(1) regardless of live count, LIFO keeps
   the working set hot) while Box::new/drop slows as the
   batch outgrows malloc's thread-cache fast path — the
   demo should show it.
5. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
6. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot_with [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Fan-in helper: consumer-side composition polling N SPSC
  rings under a pluggable service policy (priority,
  round-robin, weighted)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#fan-in-composition-not-a-mode):
  - buildable today from shipped parts;
  - likely offered alongside the MPSC ring eventually — no
    commitment yet.
- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reservation does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: mpsc ring sibling primitive [[19]]
- refactor: versioned primitive module dirs [[12]]

[12]: chores/chores-02.md#refactor-versioned-primitive-module-dirs
[19]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-mpsc-ring-sibling-primitive
[20]: chores/chores-01.md#outcome-the-2t-surprise

content.text:
The issue draft stands with two strengthenings: the repro is now *verified* on v1.10.0 (your inline-link test), and reference-style links can be mentioned as equally affected rather than being the headline. Final version:

---

**Title:** Markdown preview: cross-file anchor links open the file but discard the `#fragment`

**Body:**

### Summary

Clicking a cross-file anchor link (`[text](other.md#some-heading)`) in the Markdown preview opens the target file but never scrolls to the heading — the fragment is silently dropped. Same-file anchor links (`#some-heading`) work. Both inline links and reference-style links (`[label]` + `[label]: other.md#heading` definitions) are affected, as expected since they resolve to the same URL before click handling.

PR #53184 (which added anchor-link support, closing #18699) states cross-file anchor links should "open the file, scroll the preview, and move the editor cursor to the heading" — the scroll/cursor part doesn't happen.

### Reproduction steps

1. Create `a.md` containing: `[go to section](b.md#section-two)`
2. Create `b.md` with `# Section one`, enough lines to require scrolling, then `# Section two`
3. Open `a.md`'s Markdown preview and click the link.

### Current vs. expected behavior

- Current: `b.md` opens positioned at the top; `#section-two` is ignored.
- Expected: `b.md` opens scrolled to "Section two", editor cursor moved there (per PR #53184).

### Cause

In `crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs`, `handle_url_click` routes any link with a non-empty path part to `open_preview_url`, whose first line discards the fragment:

```rust
let (path_text, _) = split_preview_url(url.as_ref());
```

No `scroll_to_heading` is attempted after the file opens. The same-file branch (empty path part) does call `scroll_to_heading`, which is why same-file anchors work. Present in [v1.9.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L903) and [v1.10.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.10.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L1008); behavior confirmed on both.

(Possibly why it went unnoticed: the zed repo's own markdown barely uses cross-file anchors — `rg -P '\[\d+\]: '` finds no reference-definition usage outside code comments.)

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.0 (also reproduced on v1.9.0)
OS: Linux (Arch, kernel 7.1.3-arch1-1)

---

File it under your account with `gh`?

content.text:
Your regex wasn't wrong — it just answered a narrower question than the one that matters for the bug. `\[\d+\]: ` finds only *numeric* reference definitions (`[1]: url`, the style this project's notes use). Two things it can't see:

- Reference definitions with word labels — `[some-label]: url` — which are more common in the wild.
- Inline cross-file anchors — `[text](file.md#heading)` — which don't involve a definition at all, and are the primary form the bug affects.

You have a clone at `~/data/prgs/clones/zed`; let me run the broader sweeps there:

input.command: rg -P '^\s*\[[^\]^\s]+\]:\s+\S' ~/data/prgs/clones/zed --type md -c | sort -t: -k2 -rn | head; echo ---; rg -P '\]\([^)#\s]+\.md#[^)]+\)' ~/data/prgs/clones/zed --type md -c | sort -t: -k2 -rn | head

content.text:
The broader sweep refines your conclusion rather than overturning it:

- **Reference definitions** (any label, not just numeric): essentially unused — 15 hits total, and they're in an eval fixture and one README. Your regex's verdict stands; widening the label pattern didn't change it.
- **Inline cross-file anchors** (`file.md#heading`): heavily used — dozens of files under `docs/src/` with 4–22 each. So Zed's repo is *full* of exactly the link form the bug breaks.

But those live in `docs/`, which is their mdBook-published website (zed.dev/docs) — contributors read it rendered on the web, not through Zed's own markdown preview. So the dogfooding-gap explanation survives in a sharper form: it's not that they never *write* cross-file anchors, it's that they never *consume* them through the preview feature that's broken. Worth phrasing the issue's footnote that way instead of the reference-definition observation — it's both more accurate and more persuasive ("your own docs/ tree is a ready-made repro corpus").

Want me to update that footnote in the draft, and then file it?

message.content:
I'm running zed in the zed dir and the "regular" style local and external markdown links work, such as:
 [Building Zed for Windows](./docs/src/development/windows.md)

content.text:
Right — that's consistent with everything we've found. Plain cross-file links (`./docs/src/development/windows.md`, no `#fragment`) go through the same `open_preview_url` path and work, because opening the file is the part that functions. The bug is confined to exactly one thing: when a `#fragment` is present on a cross-file link, it's split off and thrown away, so you land at the top of the file instead of the section. External `https://` links take a different path entirely and are unaffected.

So the full behavior matrix in Zed 1.9/1.10 preview: same-file `#anchor` ✓, cross-file plain ✓, external ✓, cross-file `file.md#anchor` ✗ (opens, doesn't scroll) — a nicely narrow bug, which makes the one-line cause (`let (path_text, _) = ...`) very credible to a maintainer.

Shall I fold that matrix plus the refined dogfooding footnote (docs/ is full of cross-file anchors, but it's consumed via the mdBook site, not the preview) into the draft and file it under your account?

Found a perfect one in Zed's own docs — target deep enough in the file that the failure is unmistakable:

**Open `docs/src/git.md` in Zed, preview it, and go to line 208:**

> To run setup steps after Zed creates a linked worktree, use the [`create_worktree` task hook](./tasks.md#hooks).

Clicking it should scroll `tasks.md` to `## Hooks` — which is at **line 233** of that file, so if the bug fires you'll land conspicuously at the top instead. It even uses the `./`-prefixed form from PR #53184's own test plan, ruling out any path-format excuse.

Backup candidates if you want a second: `docs/src/debugger.md:89` → `./tasks.md#variables` (heading at tasks.md:82), and `git.md:350` → `./getting-started.md#command-palette`.

If it fails as predicted, that gives the issue its strongest possible repro: "click this link in your own repo's docs." Want me to swap that into the draft as the repro and file it?

input.content:
Title: Markdown preview: cross-file anchor links open the file but discard the #fragment

### Summary

Clicking a cross-file anchor link (`[text](other.md#some-heading)`) in the
Markdown preview opens the target file but never scrolls to the heading —
the fragment is silently dropped. All other link forms work: same-file
anchors (`#some-heading`) scroll correctly, plain cross-file links
(`other.md`) open correctly, and external URLs are unaffected. Both inline
links and reference-style links (`[label]` + `[label]: other.md#heading`
definitions) fail identically, as expected since they resolve to the same
URL before click handling.

PR #53184 (which added anchor-link support, closing #18699) states
cross-file anchor links should "open the file, scroll the preview, and
move the editor cursor to the heading" — the scroll/cursor part doesn't
happen.

### Reproduction steps

Reproducible with Zed's own docs:

1. Open `docs/src/git.md` from the zed repo and open its Markdown preview.
2. Click the link at line 208: `[`create_worktree` task hook](./tasks.md#hooks)`.
3. `tasks.md` opens at the top; the `## Hooks` heading (line 233) is never
   scrolled to.

Or minimally:

1. Create `a.md` containing: `[go to section](b.md#section-two)`
2. Create `b.md` with `# Section one`, enough lines to require scrolling,
   then `# Section two`
3. Open `a.md`'s Markdown preview and click the link.

### Current vs. expected behavior

- Current: the target file opens positioned at the top; the fragment is
  ignored.
- Expected: the target file opens scrolled to the heading, editor cursor
  moved there (per PR #53184's description).

### Cause

In `crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs`,
`handle_url_click` routes any link with a non-empty path part to
`open_preview_url`, whose first line splits the fragment off and discards
it:

```rust
let (path_text, _) = split_preview_url(url.as_ref());
```

No `scroll_to_heading` is attempted after the file opens. The same-file
branch (empty path part) does call `scroll_to_heading`, which is why
same-file anchors work. Present in
[v1.9.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L903)
and
[v1.10.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.10.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L1008);
behavior confirmed on both.

(Possibly why it went unnoticed: the zed repo's `docs/src/` tree is full
of cross-file anchor links, but those are consumed through the mdBook
site at zed.dev/docs rather than through the Markdown preview.)

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.0 (also reproduced on v1.9.0)
OS: Linux (Arch, kernel 7.1.3-arch1-1)

Title: Markdown preview: cross-file anchor links open the file but discard the #fragment

### Summary

Clicking a cross-file anchor link (`[text](other.md#some-heading)`) in the
Markdown preview opens the target file but never scrolls to the heading —
the fragment is silently dropped. All other link forms work: same-file
anchors (`#some-heading`) scroll correctly, plain cross-file links
(`other.md`) open correctly, and external URLs are unaffected. Both inline
links and reference-style links (`[label]` + `[label]: other.md#heading`
definitions) fail identically, as expected since they resolve to the same
URL before click handling.

PR #53184 (which added anchor-link support, closing #18699) states
cross-file anchor links should "open the file, scroll the preview, and
move the editor cursor to the heading" — the scroll/cursor part doesn't
happen.

### Reproduction steps

Reproducible with Zed's own docs:

1. Open `docs/src/git.md` from the zed repo and open its Markdown preview.
2. Click the link at line 208: `[`create_worktree` task hook](./tasks.md#hooks)`.
3. `tasks.md` opens at the top; the `## Hooks` heading (line 233) is never
   scrolled to.

Or minimally:

1. Create `a.md` containing: `[go to section](b.md#section-two)`
2. Create `b.md` with `# Section one`, enough lines to require scrolling,
   then `# Section two`
3. Open `a.md`'s Markdown preview and click the link.

### Current vs. expected behavior

- Current: the target file opens positioned at the top; the fragment is
  ignored.
- Expected: the target file opens scrolled to the heading, editor cursor
  moved there (per PR #53184's description).

### Cause

In `crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs`,
`handle_url_click` routes any link with a non-empty path part to
`open_preview_url`, whose first line splits the fragment off and discards
it:

```rust
let (path_text, _) = split_preview_url(url.as_ref());
```

No `scroll_to_heading` is attempted after the file opens. The same-file
branch (empty path part) does call `scroll_to_heading`, which is why
same-file anchors work. Present in
[v1.9.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.9.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L903)
and
[v1.10.0](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/v1.10.0/crates/markdown_preview/src/markdown_preview_view.rs#L1008);
behavior confirmed on both.

(Possibly why it went unnoticed: the zed repo's `docs/src/` tree is full
of cross-file anchor links, but those are consumed through the mdBook
site at zed.dev/docs rather than through the Markdown preview.)

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.0 (also reproduced on v1.9.0)
OS: Linux (Arch, kernel 7.1.3-arch1-1)

lines: 1. Open `docs/src/git.md` from the zed repo and open its Markdown preview.
-2. Click the link at line 208: `[`create_worktree` task hook](./tasks.md#hooks)`.
-3. `tasks.md` opens at the top; the `## Hooks` heading (line 233) is never
+3. `all-settings.md` opens at the top; the `## Git Worktree Directory`

Confirmed end-to-end, then — the draft at `~/zed-markdown-link-issue.txt` is verified and ready. File it however you prefer: paste into GitHub's new-issue form, or say the word and I'll run `gh issue create --repo zed-industries/zed` with that title and body under your account.
